title: feat(ext/crypto): JWK support for unwrapKey/wrapKey
body:
Towards #11690 - implements `jwk` format for `wrapKey`+`unwrapKey` using `TextEncoder`/`TextDecoder` for `utf-8`.

Additionally, fixes `usages` passed internally to `encrypt()`/`decrypt()` which was not working, since `[un]wrappingKey` has `[un]wrapKey` usage but `decrypt()` expects a `decrypt` usage.

Note: `subtle.encrypt()` is currently *not validating* `usages`. 

wpt(`WebCrypto/wrapKey_unwrapKey`) now passes 105/183
 - still 78 failures due to missing [`AES-KW`](#13286) and [`AES-GCM`](#13119 ) algorithms.
author: cryptographix

title: Improve DX for wpt - added debug + update-debug options
body:
There is apparently no easy method to debug WPT tests using VSCode/chrome debugger. One solution is to manually modify `tools/runner.ts` to include `--inspect-brk` (and then remove it when no longer needed .. ).

PR adds a new option to wpt.ts, `--inspect-brk`, thus avoiding the manual modifications. Applies to `run` and `update` commands.

Still not optimal DX when using `chrome://inspect/#devices`, since have to step through the top-level generated wpt-bundle that runs `Deno.core.evalContext` on the inline test-scripts, in order to set breakpoints and thus be able to single-step code.
author: cryptographix

title: refactor(core): InspectorSession implements Stream
body:
This commit rewrites "InspectorSession" to no longer implement "Future"
trait but instead implement "Stream" trait. "Stream" trait is implemented 
by yielding a raw pointer to the "v8::inspector::V8InspectorSession" and 
received message. In effect received messages are no longer dispatched 
from within the future, but are explicitly dispatched by the caller.

This change should allow us to dispatch a message to the session when
another message is being dispatched, ie. 
"V8InspectorSesssion::dispatch_protocol_message" is already on the 
call stack. This situation can happen during an execution pause, especially
in situation from #13234

title: fix: use a single file for the emit cache to eliminate multiple cache files getting out of sync
body:
Solves part of #13302.

This PR combines the version hash (`.meta`), emit (`.js`), source map (`.map`), and declaration (`.d.ts`) cache files into a single file (`.emitinfo`) to eliminate race conditions between the four files on the file system. This should now make it impossible for a version hash to be stored for an emit that doesn't match.

This does not completely solve #13302 because our emit process still relies on using cache data from the file system that could be out of date with the data stored in the graph if another deno process edits it. We should fix this part in a future PR.

At the current moment, this will be less performant because it will be loading all the data from the file when checking the version hash. This can be essentially eliminated when implementing the rest of #13302 because we'll be able to keep everything in memory instead of visiting the cache multiple times. There will only be a performance penalty when the cache is out of date instead of also when it's also not out of date.

title: fix(coverage): don't type check
body:
This commit changes "deno coverage" command not to type check.

Instead of relying on infrastructure for module loading in "deno run";
the code now directly reaches into cache for original and transpiled
sources. In case sources are not available the error is returned to the
user, suggesting to first run "deno test --coverage" command.

Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13161
Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13326
author: bartlomieju

title: fix: `deno install` with `--allow-all` should output shim with `--allow-all` instead of each permission
body:
This changes doing `deno install` with `-A`/`--allow-all` to output a shim with `--allow-all` only instead of listing every permission flag.

We do not need to be concerned about potential future permission flags because `--allow-all` has a permission for `--allow-run` which allows for anything to occur already.

Closes #11819

title: fix(coverage): merge coverage ranges
body:
Covered ranges were not merged and thus it appeared that some lines
might be uncovered. To fix this I used "v8-coverage" that takes care
of merging the ranges properly. With this change, coverage collected
from a file by multiple entrypoints is now correctly calculated. 

I ended up forking https://github.com/demurgos/v8-coverage and adding
"cli/tools/coverage/merge.rs" and "cli/tools/coverage/range_tree.rs".

- Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/10944
- Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12751
- Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/11875
- Fixes #9754
- Fixes #9865
- Fixes #13323
- Fixes #11875

title: fix(cli): fix `deno install --prompt`
body:
I noticed that `deno install --prompt` was not working. I have fixed it.

before
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.17.2 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 9.7.106.15
typescript 4.5.2

$ deno help install |grep prompt
        --prompt                                            Fallback to prompt if required permission wasn't passed

$ deno install --prompt -f --allow-read https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts

$ ~/.deno/bin/file_server
HTTP server listening on http://0.0.0.0:4507/
error: Uncaught (in promise) PermissionDenied: Requires net access to "0.0.0.0:4507", run again with the --allow-net flag
  const listener = Deno.listen(addr);
                        ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:149:12)
    at opListen (deno:ext/net/01_net.js:38:17)
    at Object.listen (deno:ext/net/01_net.js:204:17)
    at serve (https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/server.ts:304:25)
    at listenAndServe (https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/server.ts:324:18)
    at main (https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts:680:5)
    at https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts:686:3

$ cat ~/.deno/bin/file_server
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --allow-read 'https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts' "$@"
```

after
```
$ ./deno install --prompt -f --allow-read https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts

$ ~/.deno/bin/file_server
⚠️  ️Deno requests net access to "0.0.0.0:4507". Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)] y
HTTP server listening on http://0.0.0.0:4507/
[2022-01-12 10:04:31] "GET / HTTP/1.0" 200
^C

$ cat ~/.deno/bin/file_server
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --allow-read --prompt 'https://deno.land/std@0.106.0/http/file_server.ts' "$@"
```

title: refactor(lsp): remove `performance` from `StateSnapshot`
body: `Performance` was hoping along for the ride on `StateSnapshot`, but it is not really part of the concept of a "snapshot". Instead it should be a constructor dependency on these other structs.

title: refactor(lsp): store assets behind a mutex
body:
This PR stores all assests behind a mutex in order to make more language server messages not require mutability.

This is possible to do because the assets are static and it is ok if two threads race to retrieve a non-cached asset from tsc.

This is slowly moving towards #11307.
author: dsherret

title: chore: update v8 to 0.38.1
author: lucacasonato

title: refactor(lsp): store the `LspUrlMap`'s state behind a mutex
body:
Similar to #13414, this PR stores the state of `LspUrlMap` behind a mutex in order to make more language server messages not require mutability (and run concurrently in the future).

I believe this is ok to do because the `LspUrlMap` is a cache whose value doesn't change once set and providing a value to `normalize_specifier` will always have the same output. That said, `normalize_url` might be different based on whether `normalize_specifier` has been called yet or not. I think for how `LspUrlMap` is used this shouldn't matter?

Again, slowly moving towards #11307.

title: refactor(core): Extract poll_value out of resolve_value
body:
This is similar to run_event_loop X poll_event_loop. The reason for
this is that the future returned by resolve_value has the same
lifetime as the runtime. That is fine when rust can reason about it,
but in some cases it can't.

In the particular case we have, our runtime, which includes a
JsRuntime, is stored in a Rc<RefCell<...>>. With this change we can
easily poll to see if a value has been fulfilled.
author: espindola

title: fix(emit): eliminate multiple cache files getting out of sync and fix racy emit
body:
1. Uses an sqlite database file to store the emit cache to eliminate multiple cache files getting out of sync.
2. The result of checking the emit is stored in memory and if valid is kept in memory then used in the rest of the emit. This prevents consulting the file system to check if the cache is valid and then reading from the file system later when the cache may no longer be valid, which was racy.

Supersedes #13303.

Closes #13302

WIP though basically done. I could do way more cleanup and have a better design, but wanted to make the minimal changes possible.

TODO:

- [ ] Ensure all calls to `get_emit_data` verify the source. Maybe an API change would be good to force this (ex. `ProcState`'s `SourceMapGetter` impl right now retrieves the source map from the cache, but doesn't do any source checks on it). I think though it probably needs more rework in that area and I will have to read the code more.
- [ ] coverage's `cover_files` does not verify the retrieved emit text is for the source
author: dsherret

title: fix(ext/crypto): duplicate RsaHashedImportParams types
body:
Can be observed here: https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/RsaHashedImportParams
It did not break `importKey` and wasn't detected by our tests because the right definition was inferred as [HmacImportParams](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/HmacImportParams) 

(i wish lint could complaint about overriding fields in duplicate definitions)
author: littledivy

title: fix(lsp): respect DENO_CERT and other options related to TLS certs
body:
Fixes #13437

The LSP now respects `DENO_CERT` and `DENO_TLS_CA_STORE` for registry suggestions and caching remote modules.

This also adds the options to the configuration of the LSP:

- `tlsCertificate` - this mirrors the `--cert` option (and overrides the `DENO_CERT` environment variable)
- `certificateStores` - this overrides the `DENO_TLS_CA_STORE` enviornment variable.
- `unsafelyIgnoreCertificateErrors` - this mirrors the `--unsafely-ignore-certificate-errors` command line option.

It extracts the creation logic for `RootCertStore` which is used by the file fetcher to the file fetcher which is now shared between `ProcState` and the lsp.

title: feat(compat): CJS/ESM interoperability
body:
This commit adds CJS/ESM interoperability when running in --compat mode.

Before executing files, they are analyzed and all CommonJS modules are
transformed on the fly to a ES modules. This is done by utilizing `analyze_cjs()`
functionality from `deno_ast`. After discovering exports and reexports, an ES 
module is rendered and saved in memory for later use.

There's a caveat that all files ending with ".js" extension are considered as
CommonJS modules (unless there's a related "package.json" with "type": "module").

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12648
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13200
author: bartlomieju

body:
Was looking through the code for more deadlocks and did some refactoring to remove the circular dependency between `LanguageServer` and `DiagnoticsServer`. To be clear, I don't see any deadlock potential in this code, but removing the circular dependency will reduce the chance of code changes in the future causing a deadlock.

The circular dependency is removed by the language server sending a snapshot to the diagnostics server when causing a diagnostics update.
author: dsherret

title: feat(lsp): add redirect diagnostic and quick fix
body:
Ref: #12864

This is a more general solution to #12864, which I think I like better.  It also "works" for pinning URLs with the Deno warnings like `deno.land/x` and `deno.land/std`.

Basically if a specifier is redirected, a quick fix will be available to replace the import with the redirected URL.  For example:

title: feat(ext/ffi): Support read only statics
body:
This PR introduces support for read only statics binding in Deno FFI. The API is added within the existing unstable FFI symbols definition while staying backwards compatible with existing FFI definitions. This means that the API is perhaps a bit confused:

```ts
const dylib = Deno.dlopen(libPath, {
  "printSomething": {
    name: "print_something",
    parameters: [],
    result: "void",
  },
  "static_u32": {
    name: "static_u32",
    type: "u32",
  },
});
```

Statics explicitly define their type, whereas functions just barge in with their definitions just as before. This also means that the serde type for the `HashMap` that is symbols in `op_ffi_load` needs to use an untagged enum.

### Testing
* A few basic tests are added
  * u32 static
  * pointer static

### TypeScript bindings
* The bindings for the FFI API have been updated to include the possibility of native values, but have not been tested for correctness.

### Future work
Mutable statics are not supported by this PR. I see the way forward with those being an optional `mutable` flag on the static definition. If the flag is set set, then the API would use a getter and setter for the value instead of the current fetch-and-cache-once logic.

Additionally, at present the bindings do not support null symbols. This is (I presume) a reasonable choice since an immutable static symbol with a null value is forever just a null value. With mutable statics a null symbol might become more relevant, at which point it will become necessary to do something like in https://docs.rs/dlopen/latest/dlopen/raw/struct.Library.html#example-1. Allowing null symbols might also be put behind a flag if so desired. That would allow for cleaner TS typings.

Closes #13570

title: BREAKING: don't inherit permissions by default
body:
Previously specifying permissions: {} was the same as specifying
permissions: "inherit". Now it will be the same as permissions: "none".
Not specifying any permissions (permissions: undefined) still means
permissions: "inherit".

This is a breaking change. I want to try land this soon, as the current
behaviour is really impractical. If this can not be changed right now, I
am proposing it as a 2.0 change.

Was failing earlier because `undefined` is not `None` with serde (for `Option<Vec<u8>>`). This patch passes an empty buf to the op.
author: littledivy

title: feat: deno vendor
body:
Not part of this first pass:

- Warning when dynamic import is not statically analyzable (https://github.com/denoland/deno_graph/issues/138)
- Special handling of file system layout to take into account Window's 260 character path limit. I had some code that handled this, but it wasn't very good and I thought of a better solution. I'll open an issue and do another PR at a later time.
- Module specifiers with different text in the same module that resolve to the same module aren't handled (https://github.com/denoland/deno_graph/issues/132) -- Not a big deal because this is probably super rare
- Directories with different casing resolve to the same folder on case insensistive file systems. I will have an upcomming PR with the 260 char limit that fixes this (just added this to dnt [here](https://github.com/denoland/dnt/pull/125))
- The specified import map is not combined with the generated import map. It might be hard to do this correctly because the base of the import map changes when going into the vendor folder. Right now it's not a big deal for people to manually update the created import map after.

Differences with proposal:

- Uses `vendor` folder instead of `_vendor` by default (seemed like in the meeting people wanted `vendor` instead?).
- Output is not dependent on the existing state of the vendor folder.
  - Errors when the vendor folder exists and is non-empty. Allows using a `--force` flag to potentially overwrite the contents.
  - No `--info` flag
  - Specifying an import map via `--import-map ...` located within the output directory will error.
  -  Why: Vendor is already a complex feature and I think basing the vendor output on the existing state of the vendor folder would be very difficult to do. It would also be hard to fix bugs because we'd need to know the previous state of the vendor folder. I think it also might be difficult for some users to understand.

Closes #13346

title: feat(test): improved op sanitizer errors + traces
body:
This commit improves the error messages for the `deno test` async op
sanitizer. It does this in two ways:
- it uses handwritten error messages for each op that could be leaking
- it includes traces showing where each op was started

This "async op tracing" functionality is a new feature in deno_core.
It likely has a significant performance impact, which is why it is only
enabled in tests.

title: feat: "deno task" subcommand
body: __Don't expect to land it, just a quick experiment.__ _This functionality should be compatible with `scripts` in `package.json`, but it would require `deno script --config package.json ` syntax at the moment_ ## Performance Faster than [`npm scripts`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/using-npm/scripts) and [`dum`](https://github.com/egoist/dum) ``` deno on  deno_scripts [!?] via 🦕 v1.19.0 via  v17.4.0 via 🦀 v1.58.1 ❯ time dum foo $ foo $ node example_script from example [] ________________________________________________________ Executed in 53.38 millis fish external usr time 35.13 millis 0.07 millis 35.06 millis sys time 12.79 millis 1.70 millis 11.09 millis deno on  deno_scripts [!?] via 🦕 v1.19.0 via  v17.4.0 via 🦀 v1.58.1 ❯ time npm run foo > foo > node example_script from example [] ________________________________________________________ Executed in 195.03 millis fish external usr time 175.96 millis 0.06 millis 175.90 millis sys time 37.74 millis 1.84 millis 35.90 millis deno on  deno_scripts [!?] via 🦕 v1.19.0 via  v17.4.0 via 🦀 v1.58.1 ❯ time ./target/release/deno script foo from example [] ________________________________________________________ Executed in 42.95 millis fish external usr time 18.63 millis 0.07 millis 18.56 millis sys time 12.56 millis 1.81 millis 10.75 millis ``` ## Examples Example with [`docland`](https://github.com/denoland/docland): ``` // deno.json { "scripts": { "build": "deno run --config deno.jsonc --import-map import-map.json --allow-read=. --allow-write=./static --allow-net build.ts" } } ``` ``` $ deno script Available scripts: - build deno run --config deno.jsonc --import-map import-map.json --allow-read=. --allow-write=./static --allow-net build.ts $ deno script build Script build deno run --config deno.jsonc --import-map import-map.json --allow-read=. --allow-write=./static --allow-net build.ts Building deploy_doc... Fetching Deno CLI releases... Latest release: v1.19.0 Documenting latest stable APIs... Saving latest stable APIs... Documenting latest unstable APIs... Saving latest unstable APIs... Documenting lib esnext... Saving lib esnext... Documenting lib dom... Saving lib dom... Done. ``` Example with [`deno_std`](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std) ``` // deno.json { "scripts": { "node:setup": "deno run --allow-read --allow-net --allow-write node/_tools/setup.ts", "node:test": "deno test --allow-read --allow-run node/_tools/test.ts" }, "fmt": { "files": { "exclude": [ ".git", "_wasm_crypto/target", "cov", "node/_tools/versions", "node/_tools/suites", "node/integrationtest/yarn.js", "wasi/testdata" ] } }, "lint": { "files": { "exclude": [ ".git", "_wasm_crypto/target", "cov", "node/vendor", "node/_module/node_modules/", "node/_tools/versions", "node/_tools/suites", "node/integrationtest/yarn.js", "wasi/testdata" ] } } } ``` ``` $ deno script Available scripts: - node:setup deno run --allow-read --allow-net --allow-write node/_tools/setup.ts - node:test deno test --allow-read --allow-run node/_tools/test.ts $ deno script node:setup Script node:setup deno run --allow-read --allow-net --allow-write node/_tools/setup.ts Check file:///Users/ib/dev/deno_std/node/_tools/setup.ts Downloading https://nodejs.org/dist/v16.13.0/node-v16.13.0.tar.gz in path "versions/node-v16.13.0.tar.gz" ... Downloaded: https://nodejs.org/dist/v16.13.0/node-v16.13.0.tar.gz into versions/node-v16.13.0.tar.gz Decompressing node-v16.13.0.tar.gz... Cleaning up previous tests Copying test files... $ deno script node:test -- test-event-emitter-check-listener-leaks.js Script node:test deno test --allow-read --allow-run node/_tools/test.ts Check file:///Users/ib/dev/deno_std/node/_tools/test.ts running 2 tests from file:///Users/ib/dev/deno_std/node/_tools/test.ts test Node.js compatibility "parallel/test-event-emitter-check-listener-leaks.js" ... ok (551ms) test checkConfigTestFilesOrder ... ok (1ms) test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (566ms) ```

title: fix(compat): cjs/esm interop for dynamic imports
body:
This commit fixes CJS/ESM interop in compat mode for dynamically
imported modules.

"ProcState::prepare_module_load" was changed to accept a list
of "graph roots" without associated "module kind". That module kind
was always hardcoded to "ESM" which is not true for CJS/ESM interop -
a CommonJs module might be imported using "import()" function. In
such case the root of the graph should have "CommonJs" module kind
instead of "ESM".

Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13790
author: bartlomieju

title: feat(net): added reuseAddress and reusePort in Sockets API
body:
Support for **SO_REUSEADDR** socket option is requested in #1195, to avoid using different ports during tests. In order to achieve the wanted feature, it now possible to use the **SO_REUSEPORT** socket option in addition.

The implemented changes are:
1. 2 additional optional arguments for `Deno.listen` and `Deno.listenTls`, namely `reuseAddr` and `reusePort`  (one for each supported option)
2. changed the `socket2` entry to support the **SO_REUSEPORT** option
3. proposed a test to verify that the `reusePort` option works as intended

In this moment, the option is checked and set only for `op_net_listen` and `op_tls_listen`. In particular, for `op_net_listen`, changes are applied only to the `listen_tcp` method.

After testing `reuseAddr`, it has been noted that the `tokio::net::lookup_host` function use to solve the provided _hostname_, sometimes resolves the address `0.0.0.0` to `127.0.0.1`, leading to unwanted effects when the following code is used:

```
const listener1 = Deno.listen({ hostname: "127.0.0.1", port: 3500, reuseAddress: true});
const listener2 = Deno.listen({ hostname: "0.0.0.0", port: 3500, reuseAddress: true});
```
One possible solution could be using the dedicated [dns-lookup](https://docs.rs/dns-lookup/latest/dns_lookup/) package

title: feat(lsp): add testing API
body:
This is a WIP PR which will add support for a custom Testing API based around what is offered via vscode.  For clients which support the experimental `testingApi` capability, the Deno language server will process testing API requests and provide testing API notifications.

The current state is that it compiles and generally works.  It requires a client that understands the protocol.

There is still a lot to do:

- [x] Filter tests properly.
- [x] Support the "steps" API for tests.
- [ ] ~~Add redirection of stdout and stderr to the cli test APIs, as writing to stdout directly from a test will cause LSP issues.~~ will address this in a follow-up, since it is a) uncommon and b) a bit complicated to redirect to the test runner.
- [ ] ~~Implement debugging runs.~~ this is more complicated that it appeared, so will implement later
- [x] Implement converting the client config to flags/permissions.
- [x] Validate and handle validation issues with the test plan when running a module.
- [x] Better structure the output of failed test cases.
- [x] Lots of tests to test the testing API.

There are also a few things that the Testing API in vscode supports that the internal cli/test API doesn't support, that might be good ideas for the future.  The custom Testing API between Deno lsp and the client support these, so it is just a matter of adding it to the cli/test API:

- The testing API differentiates between a test error and a test failure.  For example, a test that could not be run do some sort of error, like the test factory failed for some reason is an "Error" versus that test function throwing would be a "Failure".
- The testing API for a failure or error supports an expected output versus an actual output, processing the diff inside the editor.
- The testing API supports both markdown and plaintext messages on failures and errors.
- The testing API supports a location for errors or failures to associate with the message.  This could be parsed out of a stack trace itself at the top level.
- The virtual console output (where current `TestOutput::Console(s)` gets sent to) can also associate a location with the output, meaning it could in theory link back to the console log statement that generated it in the code.

title: fix(repl): fix null eval result
body:
In inspector's protocol `undefined` value is represented as `{}` (empty object) and `null` is represented as `{"value":null}` in JSON.

serde_json doesn't distinguish between these two (`property with null value` and `absent property`) by default, so the both case becomes `value: None` (= `undefined`) at deserialization, which caused #13757

This PR changes the deserialization method for `RemoteObject`'s `value` property. Now `null` value is deserialized as `value: Some(Null)`, which works as `null` when passed back to v8, and therefore fixes #13757

title: feat(ext/net): Use Socket2 crate to create TcpListener
body:
After a first attempt in solving #1195 in #13794, it has been noticed that the **SO_REUSEADDR** option has to be defaulted to true in order to pass the `net` unit tests. In the same PR was proposed to do the porting to Socket2 and the support to the options **SO_REUSEADDR** and **SO_REUSEPORT** in two separate PR. This PR presents the TcpListener implementation based on Socket2.

It is possible to notice that:
1. the **SO_REUSEADDR** option is defaulted to true only for non-windows systems, following the implementation of `TcpListener.bind()` of [std::net](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/6343edfa11c80ffed02de7e059178d4c1637ecdb/library/std/src/sys_common/net.rs)
2. similarly, the socket type is set to `SOCK_STREAM` and the `backlog` argument of `listen()` is set to 128

These steps should produce a TcpListener equal to the one produce by the instruction `std::net::TcpListener::bind(&addr)?;`

title: feat(ext/net): add reuseAddress option for UDP
body:
This commit adds a `reuseAddress` option for UDP sockets. When this option is enabled, one can listen on an address even though it is already being listened on from a different process or thread. The new socket will steal the address from the existing socket.

On Windows and Linux this uses the `SO_REUSEADDR` option, while on other Unixes this is done with `SO_REUSEPORT`.

This behavior aligns with what libuv does.

TCP sockets still unconditionally set the `SO_REUSEADDR` flag - this behavior matches Node.js and Go. This PR does not change this behaviour.

title: feat(core): codegen ops
body:
Code-generating ops via a new `#[op]` macro avoids the overhead of the polymorphic OpTable by instead generating monomorphic v8 functions optimized for each individual op. This also provides foundations required to implement v8 Fast API and ergonomic changes such as allowing ops to have their own arity.

Deno 1.19.3 (main 34c9e160992acf77f873691b3bbfb3e5e07c63d1):

`op_baseline`:
```
test bench_op_async   ... bench:     283,582 ns/iter (+/- 4,154)
test bench_op_nop     ... bench:      50,285 ns/iter (+/- 1,574)
test bench_op_pi_json ... bench:      53,111 ns/iter (+/- 5,828)
```

`deno_common`:
```
date_now:            	n = 500000, dt = 0.030s, r = 16666667/s, t = 59ns/op
op_void_sync:        	n = 10000000, dt = 0.504s, r = 19841270/s, t = 50ns/op
op_void_async:       	n = 1000000, dt = 0.268s, r = 3731343/s, t = 268ns/op
perf_now:            	n = 500000, dt = 0.058s, r = 8620690/s, t = 116ns/op
url_parse:           	n = 50000, dt = 0.070s, r = 714286/s, t = 1400ns/op
b64_rt_long:         	n = 100, dt = 0.674s, r = 148/s, t = 6740000ns/op
b64_rt_short:        	n = 1000000, dt = 0.401s, r = 2493766/s, t = 401ns/op
read_zero:           	n = 500000, dt = 0.244s, r = 2049180/s, t = 488ns/op
write_null:          	n = 500000, dt = 0.221s, r = 2262443/s, t = 442ns/op
read_128k:           	n = 50000, dt = 6.897s, r = 7250/s, t = 137940ns/op
request_new:         	n = 500000, dt = 0.647s, r = 772798/s, t = 1294ns/op
```

This PR:

`op_baseline`:
```
test bench_op_async   ... bench:     252,617 ns/iter (+/- 11,114)
test bench_op_nop     ... bench:      18,877 ns/iter (+/- 1,674)
test bench_op_pi_json ... bench:      23,310 ns/iter (+/- 2,004)
```

`deno_common`:
```
date_now:            	n = 500000, dt = 0.030s, r = 16666667/s, t = 59ns/op
op_void_sync:        	n = 10000000, dt = 0.286s, r = 34965035/s, t = 28ns/op
op_void_async:       	n = 1000000, dt = 0.250s, r = 4000000/s, t = 250ns/op
perf_now:            	n = 500000, dt = 0.055s, r = 9090909/s, t = 110ns/op
url_parse:           	n = 50000, dt = 0.068s, r = 735294/s, t = 1360ns/op
b64_rt_long:         	n = 100, dt = 0.345s, r = 290/s, t = 3450000ns/op
b64_rt_short:        	n = 1000000, dt = 0.334s, r = 2994012/s, t = 334ns/op
read_zero:           	n = 500000, dt = 0.233s, r = 2145923/s, t = 466ns/op
write_null:          	n = 500000, dt = 0.209s, r = 2392344/s, t = 417ns/op
read_128k:           	n = 50000, dt = 6.975s, r = 7168/s, t = 139500ns/op
request_new:         	n = 500000, dt = 0.626s, r = 798722/s, t = 1252ns/op
```

* Note: async ops have other "overpowering overheads" so there is not much difference there.

## Changes
- Gets rid of `deno_core::OpTable`. ops are now seperate v8 function callbacks instead of routing through `OpTable::route_op`
- Introduces a `#[deno_op::op]` and `#[deno_op::op_async]` macro to auto generate v8 callbacks for existing ops.
- Extensions are now the only way to register new ops. 
- Changes `Extensions::ops` method. 
Why? Because `rusty_v8` wants fn items when creating a function callback. Storing ops will store the function pointer instead :-) `RegisterCtx` solves this:
```rust
Extension::builder()
  .ops(vec![
    op_nop::decl()
  ])

#[op]
fn op_nop(_: &mut OpState, _: (), _: ()) -> Result<(), AnyError> {
  Ok(())
}
```
- Introduce `RegisterCtx` enum, ops can be registered as a) a v8 function callback and b) an external reference for the callback before snapshotting. 
- Macro optimizations. Skip serialization for `a`/`b` args if the expected argument is a Wild type (`_`). Also skips `Ok()` deserialization for `Result<(), E>`
- Uses faster `v8::FunctionBuilder::data` to pass `OpState` instead of `JsRuntime::state` (rusty_v8 slots).

## Todo

- [x] Move metrics back to Rust. (idk why I moved it to JS)
- [x] Is `Deno.core.ops.op_nop` faster than `Deno.core.opSync("op_nop")`? (EDIT: nope)
- [x] Merge `#[op_async]` into `#[op]`
- [x] Op middlewares

title: refactor(core): validate promise id in refOp
body:
This PR adds the wrappers of the existing `core.refOp` and `core.unrefOp`, and checks if the given promise ids are valid.

Currently we don't check whether the promise id is valid or not. If a wrong promise id is given to `core.unrefOp`, that id remains in pending_ops vector, and causes the exit condition incorrect (Deno program can exit with valid ops still remaining).

---
This implements the idea by @andreubotella in the comment https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/12889#discussion_r756966729

title: fix(fetch): Fix uncaught rejection panic with `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming`
body:
When an exception is thrown during the processing of streaming WebAssembly, `op_wasm_streaming_abort` is called. This op calls into V8, which synchronously rejects the promise and calls into the promise rejection handler, if applicable. But calling an op borrows the isolate's `JsRuntimeState` for the duration of the op, which means it is borrowed when V8 calls into `promise_reject_callback`, which tries to borrow it again, panicking.

This change changes `op_wasm_streaming_abort` from an op to a binding (`Deno.core.abortWasmStreaming`). Although that binding must borrow the `JsRuntimeState` in order to access the `WasmStreamingResource` stored in the `OpTable`, it also takes ownership of that `WasmStreamingResource` instance, which means it can drop any borrows of the `JsRuntimeState` before calling into V8.

Fixes #13917.

title: fix(workers): Make `worker.terminate()` not block the current thread
body:
Calling `worker.terminate()` used to kill the worker's isolate and then block until the worker's thread finished. This blocks the calling thread if the worker's event loop was blocked in a sync op (as with `Deno.sleepSync`), which wasn't realized at the time, but since the worker's isolate was killed at that moment, it would not block the calling thread if the worker was in a JS endless loop.

However, in #12831, in order to work around a V8 bug, worker termination was changed to first set a signal to let the worker event loop know that termination has been requested, and only kill the isolate if the event loop has not finished after 2 seconds. However, this change kept the blocking, which meant that JS endless loops in the worker now blocked the parent for 2 seconds.

As it turns out, after #12831 it is fine to signal termination and even kill the worker's isolate without waiting for the thread to finish, so this change does that. However, that might leave the async ops that receive messages and control data from the worker pending after `worker.terminate()`, which leads to odd results from the op sanitizer. Therefore, we set up a `CancelHandler` to cancel those ops when the worker is terminated.

Fixes #13705.

title: fix(core): Don't override structured clone error messages from V8
body:
In the implementation of structured serialization in `Deno.core.serialize`, whenever there is a serialization error, an exception will be thrown with the message "Failed to serialize response", even though V8 provides a message to use in such cases. This change instead throws an exception with the V8-provided message, if there is one.

Closes #13932.

title: build: use workflows for bumping versions and cargo publishing on the CI
body:
See tools/cut_a_release.md for how this would work.

Essentially:

1. User kicks off "version_bump" workflow on branch that they want to do a release from.
    ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1609021/160151787-3e0fcdea-7861-4de7-bb4a-380534dca2a5.png)
2. A PR gets opened automatically, see https://github.com/dsherret/deno/pull/3 for an example (Note: the actual changes in there are messed up because I did a force push to my branch, but you can see from the PR body how it will look).
3. The person doing the release updates the PR if necessary and merges it.
4. After merge they kick off the "cargo_publish" workflow, which will publish all the crates and tag the repo which will kick off the release workflow which creates the draft.

Closes #13874

title: chore(wasm): Don't await on the argument to `handleWasmStreaming`
body:
`handleWasmStreaming` is the function that provides the binding with the `fetch` API needed for `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming()` and `WebAssembly.compileStreaming()`. When I implemented it in #11200, I thought V8 was calling these functions with the argument of the `WebAssembly` streaming functions, without doing any resolving, and so `handleWasmStreaming` awaits for the parameter to resolve. However, as discovered in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13917#issuecomment-1065805565, V8 does in fact resolve the parameter if it's a promise (and handles rejections arising from that).

This change removes the `async` IIFE inside `handleWasmStreaming`, letting initial errors be handled synchronously (which will however not throw synchronously from the `WebAssembly` namespace functions). Awaiting is still necessary for reading the bytes of the response, though, and so there is an `async` IIFE for that.

----------------------

This PR builds on #13925, not because the changes depend on those of that PR, but because this PR changes the stack trace in one of that PR's test outputs.

title: docs(installation): support install deno with Cask
body:
[Cask](https://github.com/axetroy/cask.rs) is a package manager I have recently written, it is decentralized, cross-platform.

> Personally, I don't like the tools such as `Homebrew`. They need to be released to `homebrew-core` and not cross-platform.

Here is the screenshot for installing Deno:

![截屏2022-03-18 01 43 37](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9758711/158863271-2e871461-95b1-4d32-a9d8-919d120f1613.png)

### What does Deno team need to do when releasing a new version?

If merge PR, there is nothing need to do when releasing a new version.

Cask will automatically fetch a list of versions from Deno repository.

All workflows are the same as before.

### Speed up installation

In order to speed up the installation, it is recommended to create a repository name `denoland/deno-cask` then create a file `Cask.toml` in the root of the repository

```toml
[package]
name = "github.com/denoland/deno"
bin = "deno"
authors = ["Deno authors <deploy@deno.com>"]
keywords = []
repository = "https://github.com/denoland/deno"
description = """
A modern runtime for JavaScript and TypeScript.
"""

[darwin]
x86_64 = { url = "https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v{version}/deno-x86_64-apple-darwin.zip" }
aarch64 = { url = "https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v{version}/deno-aarch64-apple-darwin.zip" }

[windows]
x86_64 = { url = "https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v{version}/deno-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip" }

[linux]
x86_64 = { url = "https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v{version}/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip" }
```

Of course, this is optional. After all, it has invasiveness.

title: perf(web): optimize Blob functions
body:
Improves `Blob.arrayBuffer()` by ~60% and `Blob.text()` by ~38.4%

Before:
```
blob_text_large_new: 	n = 100, dt = 0.547s, r = 183/s, t = 5470000ns/op
blob_text_large_text:	n = 100, dt = 0.375s, r = 267/s, t = 3750000ns/op
blob_text_large_buf: 	n = 100, dt = 0.217s, r = 461/s, t = 2170000ns/op
```

After:
```
blob_text_large_new: 	n = 100, dt = 0.547s, r = 183/s, t = 5470000ns/op
blob_text_large_text:	n = 100, dt = 0.231s, r = 433/s, t = 2310000ns/op
blob_text_large_buf: 	n = 100, dt = 0.095s, r = 1053/s, t = 950000ns/op
```

Instead of collecting blob parts in JS, this PR Introduces a new Rust op for reading all the blob data at once.

title: chore(fetch, mimesniff): Refactor the "extract a MIME type" algorithm
body:
`Request.prototype[_mimesniff]` and `Response.prototype[_mimesniff]` are functions that implement the "extract a MIME type" algorithm from the fetch spec. These implementations are the same line by line, though. This change deduplicates that by moving most of the implementation to a `extractMimeType` function under `ext/web`'s `01_mimesniff.js`, and simply calling that with the decoded and split headers from the `[_mimesniff]` functions.

Closes #14002.

title: feat(core): disableable extensions & ops
body:
Streamlines a common middleware pattern and provides foundations for avoiding variably sized v8::ExternalReferences & enabling fully monomorphic op callpaths

Op middleware has the final say on whether an op is enabled/disabled/overridden, otherwise the logic is:
```
op.enabled = extension.enabled && op.enabled;
```

Entire extensions can be enabled/disabled, individual ops can also toggled in the extension constructor.

Main semi-open question is if disabled ops should throw or just no-op by mapping to `op_void_a?sync` thus returning `undefined` (no-op-ing is our current behaviour so I'm going to stick with that)

title: feat(ext/ffi): support structs-by-value
body:
Adds support for passing structs-by-value as parameters to Foreign Functions, as well as to return them.

Previously, we could only pass/return structs by pointers, which works fine. But it is not suitable where we
are using, for example an existing system library.

In my use case, I'm writing bindings to [Objective C runtime](https://github.com/DjDeveloperr/deno_objc). It mostly works except for struct types which
are passed by value instead of pointer/reference in certain APIs such as in AppKit's NSWindow, certain
methods take NSRect struct by value instead of reference.

Struct types are specified using `{ struct: [...field types] }`, such as `{ struct: ["u16", "i16"] }`.

- When passing structs, we accept either pointer or Typed Arrays.
- When returning structs, `Uint8Array`.
- When using struct type for statics, it behaves same as `pointer` type since structs-by-value are not a thing. Statics are simply just dereferencing the symbol pointer, which would give the struct.
author: DjDeveloperr

title: experiment(serde_v8): derive_more enabled opaque wrappers
body:
Experiment using [derive_more](https://docs.rs/derive_more/latest/derive_more/) to help generate magic-type wrappers with mostly opaque-ish internal types, so we can swap out Vec for SmallVec for ByteStrings. Creating them via Into/From rather than explicitly wrapping their internal type.

(Note that's just as efficient, `SmallVec`s can be created from Vec's with no copying, it simply transfers ownership of the buffer on the heap)

title: feat(repl): Don't type check when importing
body:
This (very small) PR implements the change described in #13640: using `no-check` by default when the REPL is run.

Although it's just a one-line change, I do have a couple questions about my (potentially rough-around-the-edges) implementation:
1. I updated the existing `repl` flag tests to verify that when the `--no-check` flag is not supplied, it's set to `None` automatically, but there's no dedicated test case that verifies this behavior. I wasn't sure whether or not it would be good to add an entirely new test for this so I decided not to, but I'm just wondering if that would be a good idea. If desired, I'm happy to add it.

2. A few integration tests (they were all under `integration::compat`) consistently didn't pass when I ran `cargo test`. Although, I also ran them again without the change I made and the same failures seemed to happen, so I'm not sure if it's directly caused by that change. So I guess I'm just looking for a little guidance on what to do about those failures, and whether or not they're relevant to the change I made. I'm happy to provide more info about the failures (e.g. which tests failed and how) if that would help.

Thanks in advance for the review!
author: a-lafrance

title: fix: deprecate URL as the first arg of fetch
body: Deno's fetch and lib.dom's fetch have slightly different declarations. (Deno's fetch accept URL as the first arg, but lib.dom's fetch doesn't) We should align Deno's declaration to lib.dom's (spec defines first arg as `typedef (Request or USVString) RequestInfo` [ref](https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#requestinfo)) to reduce the confusion. This PR tries to deprecate Deno-only signature of fetch function. With this change, fetch with URL call gets strikethrough style in vscode like the below

title: feat(ops): #[op(unstable)]
body:
`#[op(unstable)]` tags `OpDecl::is_unstable` as `true`, thus allowing op-middleware to then disable said ops

This should make unstable checks less error prone (forgotten or overlooked during review/impl), less verbose and moderately faster (check happens at runtime init vs each call)

title: fix(runtime/permissions): Resolve executable specifiers in allowlists and queries
body:
Closes #14122.

Adds two extensions to `--allow-run` behaviour:
- When `--allow-run=foo` is specified and `foo` is found in the `PATH` at startup, `RunDescriptor::Path(which("foo"))` is added to the allowlist alongside `RunDescriptor::Name("foo")`. Currently only the latter is.
- When run permission for `foo` is queried and `foo` is found in the `PATH` at runtime, either `RunDescriptor::Path(which("foo"))` or `RunDescriptor::Name("foo")` would qualify in the allowlist. Currently only the latter does.

This results in the following outcomes for permission checks:
| `deno run --allow-run=echo`              | `which echo == "/usr/bin/echo"` at startup | `which echo != "/usr/bin/echo"` at startup |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "echo" })`**          | ✅                                          | ✅                                          |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "/usr/bin/echo" })`** | ✅                                          | ❌                                          |

| `deno run --allow-run=/usr/bin/echo`     | `which echo == "/usr/bin/echo"` at runtime | `which echo != "/usr/bin/echo"` at runtime |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "echo" })`**          | ✅                                          | ❌                                          |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "/usr/bin/echo" })`** | ✅                                          | ✅                                          |

<details>
<summary>Formerly:</summary>

| `deno run --allow-run=echo`              | `which echo == "/usr/bin/echo"` at startup | `which echo != "/usr/bin/echo"` at startup |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "echo" })`**          | ✅                                          | ✅                                          |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "/usr/bin/echo" })`** | ❌                                          | ❌                                          |

| `deno run --allow-run=/usr/bin/echo`     | `which echo == "/usr/bin/echo"` at runtime | `which echo != "/usr/bin/echo"` at runtime |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "echo" })`**          | ❌                                          | ❌                                          |
| **`Deno.run({ cmd: "/usr/bin/echo" })`** | ✅                                          | ✅                                          |
</details>
author: nayeemrmn

title: chore(core): Get rid of op ID's
body:
After the ops refactor, op ID's are only used for metrics anymore. However, the fact that there is a JS-managed `opIds` object that doesn't get updated with ops that are added after building the V8 snapshot is a problem for tsc, which uses such late op loading, although this only seems to be a problem for some build configurations and some versions of V8 (see https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/895#issuecomment-1090603520). In any case, this change is an experiment in getting rid of op ID's, and it solves that issue.

TODO: Changing `OpsTracker` to be a `HashMap` whose keys are strings might regress performance. Consider using some map with immutable entries but mutable values, and/or atomic/interned strings.

title: feat(repl): add "--eval-file" flag to execute a script file on startup
body:
Syntax

deno repl --script /path/to/script

Purpose

The purpose of this change is to provide deno with an option that is analogous to python -i /path/to/source, which runs the specified source file as additional prelude and then shows the user a REPL that can be used to enter more code. Python's -i flag is very useful for prototyping and experimentation. It is also useful for students who want to set up example structures and then examine them in an interactive session to improve their understanding of the language. This feature is different from "deno run" because "deno run" exits once the specified module exits. deno run is not interactive.

Example

``` $ cat a.js let a=5; $ deno repl --script a.js Deno 1.20.5 exit using ctrl+d or close() > a 5 > ```

Related Issues

This feature (and closely related features) have been previously discussed: 1. #3173 2. #7425 3. #7938 4. #11317 5. #13256

Related Work

Related work includes the implementation of the --eval flag (https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/11590) which allows the user to specify the prelude code as command-line parameters. This is useful, but may be inconvenient for larger preludes.

Potential Concerns

1. --script may be a bad flag name. #13256 suggests the name --eval-file. --prelude is another possibility, but it may be confusing because cli/tools/repl/session.rs already specifies some initial commands in a PRELUDE variable. 2. There is discussion by @dsherret on #13256 that this feature may not be worth it. It is already possible to expose module exports efficiently via --eval. Do we want a way to expose local variables in an interactive session? I think the answer is "probably." For example, students may find it useful to use preludes which define variables in a global scope because they don't understand modules yet. Other users may find it useful during prototyping when the amount of code is small and not worth structuring as a module. 3. There is discussion by @dsherret on #11317 about possibly modifying the script code to rewrite module import paths, etc. This implementation does not attempt to do that. 4. If the user specifies both --script and --eval, which should be executed first? In this implementation, I execute --script first. 5. Perhaps we should support multiple prelude files? The current implementation supports only one. 6. Perhaps we should support http:// in addition to file system access? 7. This is my first Rust program, so there may be bugs or non-idiomatic patterns in the code.
author: najumancheril

body: Following changes were done in this PR: - remove "test" prefix before each test - use gray color for "running N tests from ..." prompt - use relative path or remote URL instead of full URL in "running N tests from ..." prompt - in "failures" section, add file path/remote URL before the test name Before: After: Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14186 Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14188 Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14187 More PRs to follow, that will improve final report and errors display.

title: refactor: remove serde_json::Value from inspector
body:
This commit removes the `serde_json::Value` intermediary from the
`LocalInspectorSession`. This is done as preparatory work for fixing the
error that occurs when the REPL evaluates an expression that returns a
string that contains invalid surrogates sequences.

As a side-effect to the changes to the parsing of notifications, this
fixes the panic that occurs in the REPL when logging strings
containing lone surrogates. This does not yet resolve the error when a
REPL expression returns a string that contains lone surrogates.

TODO:
- [ ] add test for the thing that was fixed

Fixes #12226

title: perf(runtime): read entire files in single ops
body:
Pending on https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14221

Sync benchmarks:
```
hyperfine '../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test.js' 'node test.js' 'deno run -A --no-check test.js'
Benchmark 1: ../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test.js
  Time (mean ± σ):     163.3 ms ±   2.4 ms    [User: 11.2 ms, System: 152.9 ms]
  Range (min … max):   160.3 ms … 169.3 ms    18 runs

Benchmark 2: node test.js
  Time (mean ± σ):     171.6 ms ±   1.7 ms    [User: 19.5 ms, System: 151.7 ms]
  Range (min … max):   169.5 ms … 175.7 ms    17 runs

Benchmark 3: deno run -A --no-check test.js
  Time (mean ± σ):     172.4 ms ±   1.3 ms    [User: 19.7 ms, System: 155.7 ms]
  Range (min … max):   169.9 ms … 174.4 ms    17 runs

Summary
  '../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test.js' ran
    1.05 ± 0.02 times faster than 'node test.js'
    1.06 ± 0.02 times faster than 'deno run -A --no-check test.js'
```

Async benchmarks:
```
hyperfine '../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs' 'node test_async.mjs' 'deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs'
Benchmark 1: ../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs
  Time (mean ± σ):     308.3 ms ±   3.3 ms    [User: 11.6 ms, System: 297.8 ms]
  Range (min … max):   305.1 ms … 316.9 ms    10 runs

Benchmark 2: node test_async.mjs
  Time (mean ± σ):     363.8 ms ±   1.0 ms    [User: 40.8 ms, System: 333.1 ms]
  Range (min … max):   362.3 ms … 366.1 ms    10 runs

Benchmark 3: deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs
  Time (mean ± σ):     672.7 ms ±   5.4 ms    [User: 251.3 ms, System: 453.0 ms]
  Range (min … max):   666.1 ms … 682.2 ms    10 runs

Summary
  '../deno/target/release/deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs' ran
    1.18 ± 0.01 times faster than 'node test_async.mjs'
    2.18 ± 0.03 times faster than 'deno run -A --no-check test_async.mjs'
```

title: feat(test): format user code output
body: Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14192 This commit changes "deno test" to better denote user output coming from test cases. This is done by printing "---- output ----" and "---- output end ----" markers if an output is produced. The output from "console" and "Deno.core.print" is captured, as well as direct writes to "Deno.stdout" and "Deno.stderr". To achieve that new APIs were added to "deno_core" crate, that allow to replace an existing resource with a different one (while keeping resource ids intact). Resources for stdout and stderr are replaced by pipes.
author: bartlomieju

title: refactor: Move source map lookups to core
body: This had been blocking lots of other cleanups as well. Namely the following transformations gradually faced by `JsError` have all been moved up front to `JsError::from_v8_exception()`: finding the first non-`deno:` source line [[old code]](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/f7e7f548499eff8d2df0872d1340ddcdfa028c45/cli/source_maps.rs#L60-L80); moving `JsError::script_resource_name` etc. into the first error stack in case of syntax errors [[old code]](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/f7e7f548499eff8d2df0872d1340ddcdfa028c45/cli/fmt_errors.rs#L259-L273); source mapping `JsError::script_resource_name` etc. when wrapping the error even though the frame locations are source mapped earlier [[old code]](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/f7e7f548499eff8d2df0872d1340ddcdfa028c45/cli/main.rs#L132-L133); removing `JsError::{script_resource_name,line_number,start_column,end_column}` entirely in favour of `js_error.frames.get(0)`. We also no longer pass a js-side callback to `core/02_error.js` from cli. I avoided doing this on previous occasions because the source map lookups were in an awkward place.

title: perf(fmt/lint): incremental formatting and linting
body:
## Formatting

Before on deno_std:

```ps
> Measure-Command { deno fmt } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 929 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
          414.208
> Measure-Command { deno fmt } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 929 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         415.6013
```

After (see second run):

```ps
> Measure-Command { ../deno/target/release/deno fmt } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 929 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         415.8299
> Measure-Command { ../deno/target/release/deno fmt } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 929 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         118.6008
```

## Linting

Before on deno_std:

```
> Measure-Command { deno lint } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 873 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         464.5036
> Measure-Command { deno lint } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 873 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         461.2106
```

After (see second run... first run is just by chance faster):

```
> Measure-Command { ../deno/target/release/deno lint } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 873 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
         451.739

V:\deno_std [main ↑10 +0 ~1 -0 !]
> Measure-Command { ../deno/target/release/deno lint } | Select-Object -Property TotalMilliseconds
Checked 873 files

TotalMilliseconds
-----------------
          114.018
```

This will be a big performance improvement if people cache their DENO_DIR on the CI too (the 400ms above is with 20 cores which would be much slower with only 2 cores on GH actions). Edit: Actually, the cache would need to be keyed on the CLI version... I'm not sure how that could work.

Closes #10906

title: fix(compat): canonicalize import paths
body:
Node resolution algo "follows symlinks"
See https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/61fefe1959a8ab9e796f409346ff619c14036c81/lib/internal/modules/esm/resolve.js#L409

PNPM relies on this behavior to have a "nested node_modules" without tons of bloated duplicated packages, see https://pnpm.io/blog/2020/05/27/flat-node-modules-is-not-the-only-way for full explanation

This PR canonicalizes import path to replicate node resolve.js behavior

title: test(ext/console): Compact empty iterables when calling Deno.inspect with compact false
body:
<!--
Before submitting a PR, please read http://deno.land/manual/contributing

1. Give the PR a descriptive title.

  Examples of good title:
    - fix(std/http): Fix race condition in server
    - docs(console): Update docstrings
    - feat(doc): Handle nested reexports

  Examples of bad title:
    - fix #7123
    - update docs
    - fix bugs

2. Ensure there is a related issue and it is referenced in the PR text.
3. Ensure there are tests that cover the changes.
4. Ensure `cargo test` passes.
5. Ensure `./tools/format.js` passes without changing files.
6. Ensure `./tools/lint.js` passes.
-->
When `Deno.inspect` is called with the `compact` option set to `false`, empty iterables will still be compacted.

**Before**
```ts
Deno.inspect([], { compact: false });
// [
// 
// ]

Deno.inspect([], { compact: false, trailingComma: true });
// [
//   ,
// ]
```

**Now**
```ts
Deno.inspect([], { compact: false });
// []

Deno.inspect([], { compact: false, trailingComma: true });
// []
```

This is required to fix [this issue in denoland/deno_std](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2144).
author: bcheidemann

title: fix(ext): disable bytes mode on streams that don't handle it correctly
body:
Fixes #14219

Several ReadableStream instances were marked `type: "bytes"` which means that they're marked as supporting bring-your-own-buffer-mode, but then they did not actually do anything to support byob mode as `type: "bytes"` streams are required to do. They failed to handle closing correctly in byob mode. This PR makes them into regular non-bytes-mode ReadableStreams, which will act identically to before except in the case that `.getReader({ mode: "byob" })` is called on them,  which will now throw an error. This behavior is consistent with browsers like Chrome. Previously, that would return a ReadableStreamBYOBReader, as if it did handle byob mode, but it didn't handle end-of-file at all.

This solution (removing byob support) was picked instead of fixing the byob support because of https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14219#issuecomment-1107874094.

title: experiment(core): Move the promise ring to Rust (REBASED)
body:
Towards #13943.

---------------------------------------------------

This is a rebased version of #13906 which fully works with `deno_ops`. It also fixes the following issues with the original PR:

  - The original PR removed `promiseIdSymbol` and made it so a promise's ID isn't accessible to JS. This, however, makes `Deno.core.{un,}refOp` useless, since there is no way for the JS code calling them to access a promise's ID. In this PR, calling an async op returns a `[promise, id]` tuple, and `Deno.core.opAsync` sets that ID on the promise object with `promiseIdSymbol`, so the promise ID is once again accessible to JS code with access to that symbol.

  - The original PR also removed functionality for op call tracing from `01_core.js` but didn't implement it in Rust. This PR restores that functionality to the JS side for the time being.

  - This PR also fixes the issues in `PromiseRing::has` that I pointed out in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13906#discussion_r837142758.

  - #13861 removed the `sync_cb` callback, and moving the promise ring to Rust removes the `recv_cb` callback. With both callbacks gone, the `JsRuntime::init_cbs` method is now useless and has been removed.

  - Rather than making microtasks explicit all the time, this PR makes them explicit only inside of `JsRuntime::resolve_async_ops`. After the promises have been resolved, a microtask checkpoint is performed inside a `v8::TryCatch` so unhandled exceptions are correctly handled in the event loop.

cc @AaronO

<!--
Before submitting a PR, please read http://deno.land/manual/contributing

    - docs(console): Update docstrings

  Examples of bad title:

3. Ensure there are tests that cover the changes.

title: fix(test/bench): Accept file protocol module specifier
body:
This PR fixes problem reported on #14375 where deno cli `test` and `bench` panic if given a module specifier as file URL

On MacOs it would not panic, but it fails with error saying it's not possible to read the file.

I was able to reproduce the issue with integration tests, and they are now passing with the fix.

A note about the solution: It will work only for files as File protocol URL, but not for directories. A directory will return an error saying it's not possible to read the file. I decided not implement the directory for 2 reasons:
1 - this is the simpler approach, and it's better to increment if you folks want than removing it.
2 - this is probably how http:// and https:// will work for `test` and `bench`. I didn't tested this, but by reading the code, they will not be able to handle a directory given by URL

If you would like, I can change the approach and make so it will check the URL (only file protocol), if it's a directory then we can recursively get all files, otherwise we don't change it.

`cargo test` failed with 4 errors, but they are unrelated to my change and I believe that is some problem with my setup, since they seem related to symlinks
<details>
  <summary>See failures here</summary>
failures:

        ./cli/tests/unit/stat_test.ts
        statSyncSuccess
        lstatSyncSuccess
        statSuccess
        lstatSuccess

test result: FAILED. 1003 passed (6 steps); 4 failed; 59 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (1m42s)
</details>

code was formatted with `./tools/format.js`
`./tools/lint.js` passed successfully with no errors
closes #14375
author: sirgallifrey

title: fix(cli): add dom.extras lib
body:
Closes: #12558
Fixes: #14344

This adds the `lib.dom.extras.d.ts` file and modifies the `lib.dom.d.ts` file to include it.  This solves the problem where users use the `lib: ["dom", "deno.ns"]` configuration when compiling their isomorphic TypeScript and the distributed version of the DOM library does not include every web standard API that Deno supports, and therefore causes confusing errors to the user.

This means we do make a one line edit to the distributed `lib.dom.d.ts` to include `lib.dom.extras.d.ts`.  This means that users don't have to explicitly include `"dom.extras"` when using the library, so for most users previous errors will just "go away".

This does establish a pattern now where we can add items Deno supports in advance of the TypeScript distribution supporting it while allowing more isomorphic code to type check safely.

title: fix(vendor): do not panic on relative specifier with scheme-like folder name
body:
I tried to vendor something for debugging purposes today and got a panic with jspm because it had some relative specifiers that looked like this:

```
import _regex2 from './npm:uc.micro@1.0.6/categories/Cc/regex!cjs';
```

```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: windows x86_64
Version: 1.21.1
Args: ["C:\\Users\\david\\AppData\\Local\\bvm\\binaries\\denoland\\deno\\1.21.1\\bin\\deno.exe", "vendor", "--force", "_config.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'Could not find local path for npm:uc.micro@1/categories/Cc/regex!cjs', cli\tools\vendor\mappings.rs:143:11
```

The reason was a join was being done on `npm:uc.micro@1.0.6/categories/Cc/regex!cjs`, which would make it go absolute because "npm:" looks like a scheme.

title: feat(test): repeat test name if there's user output
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14315

This commit changes test report output to repeat test name before printing
result, but only if there's user output, denoted by markers.

```ts
$ cat foo_test.ts
Deno.test("my test", () => {
  console.log("console.log");
  console.log("console.error");
});
```

Before:
```
$ deno test foo_test.ts
Check file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo_test.ts
running 1 test from ./foo_test.ts
my test ...
------- output -------
console.log
console.error
----- output end -----
ok (6ms)

test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (18ms)

```

After:
```
Check file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo_test.ts
running 1 test from ./foo_test.ts
my test ...
------- output -------
console.log
console.error
----- output end -----
my test ... ok (6ms)

test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (18ms)

```

title: refactor(ext/url): URLPattern structured matchers
body:
This commit updates URLPattern to use a structured matcher instead of
always using a RegExp. This results in an approximately 0% change in performance
 in the cases I tested. The real value of this PR is that it allows for
 very fast matching in the upcoming URLPatternList, because this
interface can be implemented to use a radix tree matcher internally.

The new matcher still falls back to RegExp matching if the pattern is
too complicated to be handled by the simple cases (for example if
backtracking is required to match).

title: fix: regression - confirm should show prompt before input on Windows
body:
The issue here is that the `op_print` was not actually flushing the data to stdout and so the screen would hang waiting for user input without a prompt.

I'm opening this as a draft PR, because I don't know what's going on here and why this fixes the problem. Why do we use the raw handles and why does this change fix the issue to actually flush? (I will continue looking into it)

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d4ad2b809c62b59ee4c3767bf1ff096b30886bdb/runtime/ops/io.rs#L53-L54

You can see here that it's actually being flushed here in `op_print`... maybe if we use the previous code then we need to call `FlushFileBuffers` to actually flush it instead of just `flush()`? (https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/fileapi/nf-fileapi-flushfilebuffers)

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d4ad2b809c62b59ee4c3767bf1ff096b30886bdb/runtime/ops/io.rs#L436-L437

Closes #14545
author: dsherret

The test output for stdout and stderr gets collected on two separate threads via pipes. Which thread wakes up first (even with flushing) can be somewhat random especially on the CI where there are only two cores.

In order to make this less random, I separated out the stdout and stderr outputs to two separate test steps.

Additionally, there's no need to create `let ret = ()` for return value-less ops.
author: aapoalas

title: chore(serde_v8): throw error when string buffer exceeds v8 max length
body:
When using `TextDecoder` on very large data (> 512MB) Rust will panic, because the buffer passed to `v8::String::new_from_two_byte` exceeds `v8::String::max_length`.  This PR adds a more descriptive error message in case this happens.

Related to #13648

before:

```
thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', serde_v8\magic\u16string.rs:16:10
```

after:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Error serializing return: Cannot allocate String from UTF-16: buffer exceeds maximum length.
```

title: fix(test): Format thrown non-errors properly
body:
Fixes #14596.

I realised we also have this problem with exceptions in general. This only addresses it for testing. It would be good if we always applied nice formatting for `JsError::exception_message` (which is a different approach from this PR) but that would involve either moving inspect logic to core or the usual of adding a callback to core runtime options. But that callback would need to make a JS call to `Deno[Deno.internal].inspectArgs([error])`. I guess the callback could be registered on the JS-side, making it a lot easier? I have to look into it.

title: perf(core): optimize encode on large strings
body:
Follow up to serde_v8's #14450

```
Before:
test bench_utf8_decode_12_b  ... bench:         188 ns/iter (+/- 41)
test bench_utf8_decode_12_kb ... bench:       3,480 ns/iter (+/- 2,226)
test bench_utf8_decode_12_mb ... bench:   1,549,520 ns/iter (+/- 210,202)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_b  ... bench:         400 ns/iter (+/- 69)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_kb ... bench:       5,813 ns/iter (+/- 469)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_mb ... bench:   5,149,035 ns/iter (+/- 160,745)

After:
test bench_utf8_decode_12_b  ... bench:         174 ns/iter (+/- 13)
test bench_utf8_decode_12_kb ... bench:       3,780 ns/iter (+/- 688)
test bench_utf8_decode_12_mb ... bench:   1,484,499 ns/iter (+/- 42,377)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_b  ... bench:         345 ns/iter (+/- 22)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_kb ... bench:       2,127 ns/iter (+/- 132)
test bench_utf8_encode_12_mb ... bench:   1,198,063 ns/iter (+/- 83,304)
```

We observe non-linear improvements as input string length grows, approaching ~3x for multi-kb strings and beyond

title: fix(cli/dts): change `ChildStatus.signal` from `string` to `Deno.Signal`
body:
Since #14643 the `ChildStatus.signal` changed from `number` to `string` which is great, but a more accurate typing would be `Deno.Signal`

Currently if you try to pass it down to a type/interface which expect a `Deno.Signal`, you must force the type assertion to avoid TypeScript complaining:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22963968/169625139-0a724b6e-e229-4fdd-bbc5-3ecd1f53b5aa.png)

Not complaining with assertion:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22963968/169625168-66a9c1d9-2576-4861-903d-61bcffc318f1.png)

```
Executed in    1.98 secs      fish           external

```

title: Create root directory implicitly if `DENO_INSTALL_ROOT` does not exist
body:
If you install deno and attempt to install something when the deno root does not exist and is set with `DENO_INSTALL_ROOT` you get an incredibly unhelpful error:

    error: No such file or directory (os error 2)

This is because inside the `resolve_shim_data` call an attempt is made to canonicalize the path which doesn't yet exist.

I'm not sure this PR is the right approach to fix the issue, it might be better to try and replicate what I assume happens elsewhere in the codebase to ensure `.deno` exists, because I can't replicate this issue when I unset `DENO_INSTALL_ROOT`

title: fix: read raw stdin to prevent buffering (regression)
body:
A test for this started failing in the deno_task repo with the latest Deno. This is an edge case that probably won't affect anyone.

It turns out, Rust is buffering `std::io::stdin()` and we don't want that, so use raw stdin (unlike stdout and stderr where we want to use rust's helpers).

title: refactor: Allocate IDs for tests
body:
Blocked on #14757.

Allows for better state-keeping of tests in Rust:
- Prints ` ... cancelled` for tests that were registered and cancelled due to uncaught errors:
  ```
  running 3 tests from ./temp.ts
  testWithUncaughtRejection ...
  Uncaught error from ./temp.ts FAILED
  testWithUncaughtRejection ... cancelled (0ms)
  anotherTest ... cancelled (0ms)
  anotherOne ... cancelled (0ms)
  ```
- Always know whether or not to reprint `someTest ...` when a result arrives (addresses https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14315#issuecomment-1138141249).
- Allows lots of future improvements to output e.g. more self-contained result lines for concurrent runs:
  ```
  (./temp.ts) t1 ... ok
  (./temp.ts) t2 ... t2-step1 ... FAILED
  (./temp.ts) t2 ... t2-step2 ... ok
  (./temp.ts) t2 ... FAILED
  ```

Adds `op_register_test()` to register tests, allocate IDs to them and potentially other info:
- Move filtering to rust, `{ id, filteredOut }` is returned by this op.
-  Tests are known about in Rust earlier, allowing for the `... cancelled` output above for tests that haven't started yet.

Cleanups in LSP test runner and more functional approach in `40_testing.js`. Bring bench runner error formatting up to date, it was still using `formatError()` in JS.

title: feat(core): Add support for async ops in realms
body:
This is one proposal to support async ops in realms, that does not need the promise ring to be moved to Rust (see denoland/deno#14396). I will also submit another PR that does depend on the Rust promise ring, so they can be compared.

------------------

Pull request denoland/deno#14019 enabled initial support for realms, but it did not include support for async ops anywhere other than the main realm. The main issue was that the `js_recv_cb` callback, which resolves promises corresponding to async ops, was only set for the main realm, so async ops in other realms would never resolve. Furthermore, promise ID's are specific to each realm, which meant that async ops from other realms would result in a wrong promise from the main realm being resolved.

This change creates a `ContextState` struct, similar to `JsRuntimeState` but stored in a slot of each `v8::Context`, which contains a `js_recv_cb` callback for each realm. Combined with a new list of known realms, which stores them as `v8::Weak<v8::Context>`, and a change in the `#[op]` macro to pass the current context to `queue_async_op`, this makes it possible to send the results of promises for different realms to their realm, and prevent the ID's from getting mixed up.

Additionally, since promise ID's are no longer unique to the isolate, having a single set of unrefed ops doesn't work. This change therefore also moves `unrefed_ops` from `JsRuntimeState` to `ContextState`, and adds the lengths of the unrefed op sets for all known realms to get the total number of unrefed ops to compare in the event loop.

Towards denoland/deno_core#911.

title: feat(ext/crypto): export elliptic keys as "raw"
body:
This pull request chips away at the WebCrypto roadmap (#11690). It adds support for the "raw" format when exporting public ECDH/ECDSA keys via the SubtleCrypto.exportKey method.

The WebCrypto specification [explicitly states](https://www.w3.org/TR/WebCryptoAPI/#SubtleCrypto-method-exportKey) that support for the "raw" format is encouraged for interoperability.

As a newbie contributor, are there tests for ext/crypto? I could not find any.

title: chore: fix flaky `integration::bench::multifile_summary` test
body:
Fixes #14732. The test is flaky because sometimes "parse url 6x" runs faster than "parse url 2x". I think it may be because the execution time difference between the two benches is quite small and if a CI runner has a small performance dip, it results in the parse url 2x being slower than the parse url 6x on occasion. 

This PR changes the 6x to 200x creating a bigger difference. An alternative could also be to change the slower/faster to wildcards instead.

title: fix(lsp): handle get diagnostic errors better
body:
This handles errors in `getDiagnostics` request where there are transient errors in getting the diagnostics which cause an output of:

```
ERROR TSLS = {}
```

In the debug console, which is distracting to users and doesn't impact the usage of the extension.  Also, when one specifier causes an issue, all requested specifiers get dropped, which isn't ideal.  This will handle the whole failure of getting diagnostics better.

title: feat: deno run --watch doesn't exit on Deno.exit()
body:
This PR changes behavior of `Deno.exit()` when `--watch` flag is passed. 
Instead of exiting the watcher process, it terminates JS execution but 
keeps file watcher alive. 

This is done, by terminating JS execution from an "op_exit" instead of
calling "std::process::exit()". In such case the event loop terminates
immediately, but the flow of the Rust program continues - exit code
is grabbed from worker's state and handled by the caller. For "deno run"
subcommand it is used in "std::process::exit()" call, but for
"deno run --watch" it is handled by file watcher to display what was the
exit code and keep watching for file changes. This greatly improves
DX when quickly iterating on code. 

As a consequence "Drop" handlers are properly run which should
help alleviate several reported issues. This will be done in follow up
commits.

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/7590

title: refactor(core): Move Deno.core bindings to ops
body:
Move most `Deno.core` bindings which are defined in Rust to ops e.g. `Deno.core.encode(foo)` -> `Deno.core.opSync("op_encode", foo)` using `#[op(v8)]`. Ref #14574, #14582. One more change to the macro was required to avoid late-bound lifetime parameters when returning a value with a lifetime compatible based on `scope`'s.

The following which are used in std are preserved as wrappers in `01_core.js`, we should deprecate this syntax and update them to ops:
- `Deno.core.setMacrotaskCallback()`
- `Deno.core.setNextTickCallback()`
- `Deno.core.runMicrotasks()`
- `Deno.core.hasTickScheduled()`
- `Deno.core.setHasTickScheduled()`
- `Deno.core.evalContext()`
- `Deno.core.encode()`
